PLAINTIFF JOAQUIN TALAVERA-ZELEDON'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS (ADDED FOR POSTING OF TENTATIVE RULING)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery And For Monetary Sanctions: Points And Authoriites; Declation Of Robert Beckerman, Esq.; Seperate Statement; Proposed Order
Off Calendar Jun-27-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: PLAINTIFF JOAQUIN TALAVERA-ZELEDON's And Motion To Compel Further Discovery And For Monetary Sanctions: Points And Authoriites.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. In this motor vehicle injury lawsuit, plaintiff propounded both form interrogatories and a request for production of documents. Upon reviewing defendants' responses, plaintiff disputed the sufficiency of two related discovery responses: a form interrogatory (16.9) inquiring whether defendant possessed insurance industry index reports regarding prior or subsequent injury claims by the plaintiff and a document production request (No. 8) seeking disclosure of those same index reports. Plaintiff entered into meet and confer efforts with defense counsel, challenging the legitimacy of defendant's refusal to consider discovery of relevant index reports on the grounds of privilege: work product, attorney-client and individual and third-party privacy. Although hardly a model of meet and confer efforts, defendant remained unwilling to consider withdrawing his objections. At that juncture, plaintiff filed the pending motion to compel further discovery responses. After the filing of the motion to compel, defendant evidently reconsidered his position and provided revised responses to Interrogatory 16.9 and document request No. 8. Rather than maintain his position that the interrogatory inquired into privileged communications, defendant stated "no" in response to No. 16.9. As for document request No. 8, defendant produced an index report as to plaintiff's prior and subsequent injury claims. Although plaintiff declared himself satisfied with these supplementary responses, leaving only the issue of sanctions for resolution by the Court, he declined defendant's offer of $375 in sanctions in exchange for withdrawal of the motion to compel. As to defendant's changed position with respect to Interrogatory 16.9, the decision to invoke privilege in defendant's initial response when a simple "no" would have sufficed smacks of gamesmanship, especially since the Court can see no basis for the privilege objections in the first place. By the same token, plaintiff's argument that a privilege log was required does not follow the relevant language of the Code. CCP section 2031.240(c)(1) provides that a privilege log may be of assistance but is not always required. See also Catalina Island Yacht Club v. Superior Court, 242 Cal.App.4th 1116 (2015) (court cannot compel production of documents, only order submission of privilege log).
Request No. 8, as phrased, borders on the unintelligible. The phrase "relating to the INCIDENT" by definition eliminates the possible existence of reports of prior or subsequent injury claims, and "or any party in this case" simply makes no sense in the context of this case. Be that as it may, defendant produced an index report about any claims by plaintiff which apparently satisfied plaintiff. In sum, plaintiff had good cause to file his motion to compel, at least with respect to Interrogatory No. 16.9. His meet and confer efforts were enough to satisfy that obligation on his part. And defendant's discovery dance was essentially without substantial justification, as he eventually realized. For that reason, plaintiff's request for sanctions is GRANTED, in the amount offered by defendant -- $375.00.

Plaintiff's counsel shall prepare a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling and forward it to psw@hassard.com for review and execution.

Judge Pro Tem: Philip Ward. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (302/JPT)
